experiment: render tinygrad-native fp16 workgroup reduction (a*b).sum(axis=1) -> capture compiled code object -> llvm-objdump

findings.lds_staging: NATIVE -- tinygrad reduce kernel emits ds_load x4 + ds_store x1 (LDS tree reduction). has_lds=TRUE.
findings.cross_lane_reduction: NOT NATIVE -- NO ds_bpermute / ds_swizzle / DPP. tinygrad uses LDS tree-reduce, not warp-shuffle. has_cross_lane=FALSE.
findings.v_dot2_fused_dot: NOT NATIVE -- NO v_dot2; the fp16 multiply-add lowers to v_pk_add_f16 (packed add) + separate multiply. has_vector_dot=FALSE.

conclusion: The owned attention tile's two distinguishing ISA primitives -- v_dot2 (fused fp16 dot) and ds_bpermute (cross-lane warp reduce) -- are EXACTLY the ones tinygrad-native codegen does NOT emit (it uses LDS tree-reduce + packed add instead). LDS staging IS already native. So the native-codegen learning target is concrete and ISA-evidenced: teach the renderer cross-lane reductions and v_dot2 lowering. This is expressibility evidence, NOT a W==D claim.
method: DEV=AMD, monkeypatch Device['AMD'].compiler.compile to capture .lib bytes -> /tmp/tg_k*.co -> /opt/rocm/llvm/bin/llvm-objdump -d
